mapping.a_element: sample/shrinkage covariance matrix whose spectrum is analyzed
mapping.b_element: Grover-walk / quantum-graph operator whose spectrum is analyzed

mapping.a_element: estimation noise between overlapping windows treated as a small perturbation of the covariance
mapping.b_element: magnetic vector potential treated as a small perturbation of the periodic Grover walk

mapping.a_element: first-order perturbation of the dominant eigenprojector governing projector movement, with the eigenvalue gap (Davis-Kahan) controlling stability
mapping.b_element: first-order perturbation response whose behavior hinges on the graph's spectral structure, specifically the presence of a non-simple (degenerate) eigenvalue

brief_justification: The only genuine overlap is the shared use of first-order spectral perturbation theory (perturbing a matrix and tracking its eigenspaces, with eigenvalue gap/degeneracy governing the response), which is a generic mathematical toolbox rather than a shared transferable mechanism. The governing processes diverge decisively: paper A calibrates the statistical law of estimation-noise-induced spectral fluctuations for hypothesis testing (null distributions, Davis-Kahan bands, bootstrap, power analysis, bias debiasing), whereas paper B uses degenerate perturbation theory to derive an effective Hermitian matrix that generates continuous-time quantum-walk dynamics and characterizes robustness of periodicity — statistical inference versus deterministic quantum dynamics, with no role-for-role correspondence for A's calibration machinery or B's dynamical generator.
